did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used the Triage AI application, which employs a pretrained convolutional neural network (CNN) combined with a reinforcement learning agent, to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions as benign or malignant. The AI's performance was evaluated against medical providers using the MClass-D test set from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It describes a comparative study evaluating the performance of an AI application (Triage) against medical providers (dermatologists, family physicians, and mid-level providers) using the MClass-D test set. The paper includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results (with statistical analysis and performance metrics like sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, and NPV), and a Discussion/Conclusion, which are characteristic of original experimental research rather than a review.

title: Artificial Intelligence vs Medical Providers in the Dermoscopic Diagnosis of Melanoma
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of the Triage AI application, which uses a pretrained convolutional neural network combined with a reinforcement learning agent, in classifying dermoscopic images of skin lesions as benign or malignant. The AI was tested against 23 board-certified dermatologists, 7 family physicians, and 12 primary care mid-level providers using the MClass-D test set from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, which comprised 80 benign nevi and 20 biopsy-verified malignant melanomas. The AI achieved a sensitivity of 80%, specificity of 95%, accuracy of 92%, positive predictive value of 80%, and negative predictive value of 95%. The AI performed significantly better than all medical provider groups combined in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity (P<.05), and significantly outperformed dermatologists in specificity, accuracy, PPV, and NPV. Although there was no significant difference in sensitivity between the AI and dermatologists, the AI demonstrated superior overall diagnostic reliability for melanoma screening. The results suggest that this AI application could serve as a reliable screening tool to assist medical providers in triaging potential melanoma cases.

all_task_types.details: The AI application classifies dermoscopic images of skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories: benign (benign nevi) or malignant (biopsy-verified malignant melanomas). The study evaluates performance using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV) for this binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: MClass-D test set
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, used as the primary test set for evaluating the AI application and medical providers. The data is available via an open-source online data repository hosted at Mendeley Data.

use.testing: Used as the test set where 23 dermatologists, 7 family physicians, and 12 primary care mid-level providers labeled the images as benign or malignant, which were then compared to the AI application's performance.

datasets_used.name: Triage AI Training Data (Crowdsourced)
datasets_used.description: A diverse dataset used to train the Triage AI application's dermoscopy classifier. It consists of case photographs tagged with metadata (age, sex, symptoms, diagnoses) contributed by over 150,000 patients and 10,000 clinicians in more than 150 countries over 20+ years. All crowdsourced images used for training the dermoscopy classifier are biopsy-verified.

use.training: Used to train the convolutional neural network (CNN) and reinforcement learning agent of the Triage AI application. The data includes more than 20 years of clinical encounters and user-uploaded cases.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine statistical significance when comparing the AI application's performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) against medical providers (dermatologists, family physicians, and mid-level providers).
Significance threshold for determining statistical significance in the comparison of AI performance versus medical providers.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the AI application's performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, NPV) and those of the medical providers.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The AI application's performance metrics are significantly different from (specifically better than) those of the medical providers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using a 1-sample t test with P<.05 considered significant. The AI performed significantly better than all medical providers combined in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity (P<.05), and significantly outperformed dermatologists in specificity, accuracy, PPV, and NPV (P<.05). There was no significant difference in sensitivity between the AI and dermatologists. The AI also performed significantly better than primary care providers (P<.05).

MClass-D test set.notes: The AI performed significantly better than all medical providers combined in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity (P<.05). Compared with dermatologists, the AI was significantly more specific and accurate and demonstrated a higher PPV and NPV (P<.05). There was no significant difference between the AI and dermatologists in sensitivity. The AI performed significantly better than primary care providers (P<.05). When compared with family physicians alone, the AI did not demonstrate a statistically significant difference in sensitivity. The accuracy of individual medical providers ranged from 32% to 78%.

architecture_used.main_model: Triage AI application, which combines a pretrained convolutional neural network (CNN) with a reinforcement learning agent as a question-answering model.
architecture_used.pretraining: The CNN was pretrained on a diverse dataset including more than 20 years of clinical encounters and user-uploaded cases submitted by more than 150,000 patients and 10,000 clinicians in more than 150 countries. All crowdsourced images used for training the dermoscopy classifier are biopsy-verified. The CNN algorithm covers 133 skin disease classes, representing 588 clinical conditions.

components: Gated-logic visual classifier to reject images without visible skin conditions.
Skin classifier predicting probability of 1 of 133 classes of skin conditions (7 diagnosable via dermoscopy).
Reinforcement learning agent acting as a question-answering model to refine predictions and encode dermatology knowledge.
training_techniques: Additional weights applied to mistakes made on underrepresented classes to address class imbalances.
Data augmentation techniques including rotating, zooming, and flipping training images.

training_details.dataset_size: More than 150,000 patients and 10,000 clinicians over 20+ years.
training_details.dermoscopic_categories: 7 malignant, premalignant, and benign dermoscopic categories.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Top k most probable conditions displayed to the user (k≤5).
classification_or_output_head.refinement: Final predictions refined by a question-and-answer system encoding dermatology knowledge.

preprocessing_techniques: Gated-logic visual classifier to reject images without visible skin conditions
Data augmentation techniques including rotating, zooming, and flipping training images
Application of additional weights to mistakes made on underrepresented classes to address class imbalances

conclusions: The Triage AI application achieved a sensitivity of 80%, specificity of 95%, accuracy of 92%, positive predictive value (PPV) of 80%, and negative predictive value (NPV) of 95% in classifying dermoscopic images as benign or malignant.
The AI application performed significantly better than all medical providers combined in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity (P<.05).
Compared with board-certified dermatologists, the AI was significantly more specific and accurate and demonstrated a higher PPV and NPV (P<.05), with no significant difference in sensitivity.
The AI performed significantly better than primary care providers (family physicians and mid-level providers) in all examined categories (P<.05).
When compared with family physicians alone, the AI did not demonstrate a statistically significant difference in sensitivity.
The high accuracy and PPV of the AI application suggest it could serve as a reliable melanoma screening tool for providers.
The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the small sample size of medical providers.
Further studies are necessary to determine if the high diagnostic accuracy of the AI application translates to expedited referrals and a decrease in unnecessary biopsies.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size of medical providers (n=42 total), which may affect the generalizability of the results.
There is a potential conflict of interest as two authors (Izhaar Tejani and Tory Jarmain) are paid employees of Triage Technologies Inc, the manufacturer of the AI application studied.
The study used dermoscopic images rather than gross examination, which may not reflect real-world primary care settings where dermoscopy training is often lacking or non-standard.

There was no correlation found between years of dermatologic experience and performance in determining malignancy, suggesting that experience alone does not guarantee better dermoscopic diagnosis accuracy.

The AI was trained on a diverse, crowdsourced dataset with biopsy-verified images from over 150,000 patients and 10,000 clinicians, which may introduce selection bias compared to the controlled test set.
The study did not assess whether the high diagnostic accuracy of the AI translates to clinical outcomes such as expedited referrals or a decrease in unnecessary biopsies.
The AI application has received Health Canada Class I medical device approval and the CE mark, indicating regulatory status but not necessarily clinical efficacy in diverse real-world populations.
